front: What are DRY module patterns in Terraform and how do you structure a data platform repo?
back:
DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) Terraform uses modules to share configuration and Terragrunt or workspace patterns to manage environments without duplicating code.

Repo structure (industry standard for data platforms):
```
terraform/
  modules/
    s3-data-lake/        # reusable S3 + lifecycle + encryption
    rds-postgres/        # RDS with standard backup/security defaults
    emr-cluster/         # EMR with cost controls baked in
    airflow-mwaa/        # MWAA environment
    vpc-standard/        # VPC with standard subnet layout
  environments/
    dev/
      main.tf            # calls modules with dev-specific vars
      terraform.tfvars
    staging/
      main.tf
      terraform.tfvars
    prod/
      main.tf
      terraform.tfvars
  shared/
    state-backend/       # bootstrap: S3 + DynamoDB for remote state
```

Module versioning:
```hcl
# Pin module versions like you pin providers
module "data_lake" {
  source  = "git::https://github.com/myorg/tf-modules.git//s3-data-lake?ref=v1.3.0"
  # OR from local path during development:
  source = "../modules/s3-data-lake"
}
```

Terragrunt (optional but popular at larger orgs):
- Generates backend config per env automatically
- Enforces module versioning
- Eliminates copy-paste between environment dirs
- Common at $200K+ DE roles; worth knowing exists

Interviewer signal: candidates who describe repo structure show real production experience.
